title: Business Processes and Systems Thinking
summary: Systems thinking, business process management (BPM), BPMN notation, and the role of information systems in optimising organisational processes.
content_html:
Business Processes and Systems Thinking

Systems thinking is making sense of a complex situation by breaking it down into parts and relationships. It is a key tool underpinning all information systems work.

Systems Thinking Basics

  1. Define the boundary of the system (what is inside, what is outside).
  2. Define the objects, parts or things inside the system (people, organisational units, computers, activities).
  3. Show how the parts are related (flows of information, social relationships, reporting lines).

What is a Business Process?

A business process is a collection of linked tasks which end in the delivery of a service or product to a client. Thinking in terms of processes (rather than tasks) provides a holistic view focusing on flows of information, resources, and outputs.

Categories of Business Processes

By scope of actors:

  • Business Unit Specific: Tactical activities limited to a single unit. Risk: islands of automation.
  • Cross/Inter-Functional: Activities crossing divisional boundaries.
  • Inter-Organisational: Sharing data across two or more organisations.

By managerial task:

  • Strategic Processes: Plan, coordinate, measure and control business performance (governance, strategic planning).
  • Management/Reporting Processes: Support at BU, cross-BU, and project levels.
  • Operational Processes: Essential day-to-day business activities.

Business Process Management (BPM)

BPM is a systematic approach to improving an organisation's processes. It helps reveal information bottlenecks, redundancies, and efficiency opportunities.

BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation)

BPMN is a standard graphical notation for modelling business processes. Key symbols include:

  • Events: Start, intermediate, end (circles).
  • Activities/Tasks: Rounded rectangles representing work performed.
  • Gateways: Diamonds representing decision points (exclusive, parallel, inclusive).
  • Sequence flows: Arrows showing the order of activities.
  • Swim lanes: Pools and lanes showing which actor performs which activity.

Organisational Levels

Three primary levels require different information for decision-making:

  • Operational: Day-to-day transactions (structured decisions, detailed data).
  • Management: Monitoring and controlling (semi-structured decisions, summarised data).
  • Strategic: Long-term planning (unstructured decisions, external and aggregated data).

exercises.title: Business process definition
exercises.prompt: A business process is best defined as:
options: A single task performed by one employee
A collection of linked tasks ending in delivery of a service or product to a client
An organisational chart showing reporting lines
A software application for managing data
exercises.answer: A collection of linked tasks ending in delivery of a service or product to a client

exercises.title: BPMN gateway symbol
exercises.prompt: In BPMN, a diamond shape represents:

exercises.title: Systems thinking boundary
exercises.prompt: The first step in systems thinking is to:
options: Define the boundary of the system
exercises.answer: Define the boundary of the system

exercises.title: Process category
exercises.prompt: A supply chain management process that involves multiple organisations is classified as:

exercises.title: Digital age process value
exercises.prompt: Why is process thinking more important in the digital age than in the paper-based paradigm?
options: Digital processes allow for more paperwork
It helps understand the business in terms of flows of information, revealing bottlenecks and redundancies
Managers prefer process charts to org charts
exercises.answer: It helps understand the business in terms of flows of information, revealing bottlenecks and redundancies

exercises.title: Organisational level
exercises.prompt: Senior executives deciding on a 5-year digital strategy are operating at which organisational level?

exercises.title: Islands of automation
exercises.prompt: The risk of business unit-specific processes is:
options: Too much data sharing between units
Islands of automation -- isolated systems that don't connect
exercises.answer: Islands of automation -- isolated systems that don't connect

exercises.title: BPM purpose
exercises.prompt: Business Process Management (BPM) primarily helps organisations to:
options: Reveal information bottlenecks, redundancies, and efficiency opportunities
exercises.answer: Reveal information bottlenecks, redundancies, and efficiency opportunities
